claim: AlphaFold2's breakthrough relied heavily on exploiting evolutionary co-evolution signals in sequences—mutations that occur together across species often indicate spatial proximity in 3D structure, giving models a powerful inductive bias
actionable: When applying structure prediction to proteins with rich evolutionary history, leverage multiple sequence alignments (MSA) as a strong hint about which residue pairs are likely close in 3D space.

claim: AlphaFold2 was a regression problem (predicting a single answer), while AlphaFold3 shifted to generative modeling (sampling from a distribution), which better captures protein dynamics and uncertainty in multi-state systems
actionable: For molecular systems with multiple possible conformations, use generative models that sample distributions rather than regression models that average predictions.

claim: Boltzmann trained a large protein folding model only once due to compute constraints, fixing bugs mid-training and restarting from checkpoints rather than from scratch—the resulting model learned from a non-standard curriculum
actionable: When compute is extremely limited, iteratively fix bugs and continue training from intermediate checkpoints, but be aware this introduces curriculum learning artifacts that may be difficult to reproduce.

claim: Despite smaller parameter counts (AlphaFold2 ~70-100M parameters), structure prediction models use cubic-scaling operations on pairwise interactions rather than quadratic, resulting in higher FLOPs-to-parameters ratios than LLMs
actionable: Do not directly compare model size (parameters) between protein structure models and LLMs; protein models are more compute-intensive per parameter due to pairwise interaction modeling.

claim: Triangle layers and pairwise-focused attention in structure models persist as superior to generic transformers despite broader industry trend toward simpler architectures—specialized inductive biases remain critical for biology
actionable: For molecular structure prediction, prefer specialized architectures with pairwise (2D) representations over fully generic transformer stacks; domain-specific inductive bias still outperforms scale-only approaches.

claim: AlphaFold3 introduced atomic-level and token-level (amino acid) multi-granularity modeling, allowing the model to reason at different scales and improve performance on small molecules and RNA/DNA interactions
actionable: Use coarse-to-fine granularity in molecular design models: reason at amino-acid/token level for sequence decisions, then refine to atomic coordinates for precise interaction geometry.

claim: Boltzmann's protein design (BoltzGen) unified structure and sequence prediction into a single supervision signal by encoding both atoms and their identities jointly, rather than treating them as separate discrete/continuous tasks
actionable: When designing new proteins, merge structure and sequence supervision: use atomic coordinates as the single source of truth, inferring amino acid identity from atom placement rather than multi-headed losses.

claim: Inference-time scaling via ensembling is powerful for protein design: generate thousands of candidates, rank them with a scoring model, and iterate sampling—better ranking beats single-pass generation
actionable: For molecular design, generate many candidates in parallel and rank with a dedicated scoring model rather than relying on single generation pass; more samples + better ranking > single high-quality output.

claim: Community-discovered engineering hack: conditioning AlphaFold on random residue hints (scanning every Nth residue) helps fix stuck predictions in antibody-antigen docking, enabling inference-time search without retraining
actionable: For stuck predictions on antibody-antigen or similar interactions, condition the model on residue-level hints at inference time (e.g., 'bind residue 1, 11, 21...') to escape local optima.

claim: Validation across diverse targets and modalities (peptides, nanobodies, small molecules, disordered proteins) is more informative than single-domain testing; two-thirds success rate on unseen targets indicates real generalization, not training set regurgitation
actionable: Validate protein design models on targets with zero training-set interactions; measure success across at least 8-10 independent labs and 3+ protein modalities for credible generalization claims.

claim: Diffusion models for protein design can condition on both target structure and design specifications (e.g., 'antibody framework + small peptide binder'), jointly predicting both structure and sequence in a single pass
actionable: For guided protein design, condition diffusion models on target protein structure and high-level specs (framework type, size constraints); model outputs both 3D structure and amino acid sequence jointly.

claim: Confidence scores from structure prediction models (e.g., pLDDT) are poor proxies for affinity prediction; separate affinity models are needed to rank designs effectively after generating candidates
actionable: Do not use model confidence (pLDDT) as a proxy for binding strength; train or use separate affinity prediction models to rank generated protein designs.

claim: Boltzmann Lab uses GPU fleet amortization to make parallel screening (100k candidates) cheaper than individual users running open-source models; 10x speedup on small-molecule screening via infrastructure optimization
actionable: For large-scale molecular screening, use cloud-hosted platforms with shared GPU fleets rather than on-premise infrastructure; economies of scale make central screening 10x faster and cheaper.

claim: AlphaFold3 was not open-sourced; the gap created demand for open alternatives like Boltzmann, enabling rapid community adoption and feedback that would not have happened without open-source availability
actionable: Consider open-sourcing foundational models in high-impact domains; restricted models may drive parallel development efforts and limit field-wide progress despite commercial advantages.

claim: Recycling (iterating model inference 3-4x) on pairwise distance matrices acts as early reasoning: refining rough structure estimates from MSA/templates before final atomic positioning improves accuracy without retraining
actionable: Implement recycling loops in structure models: feed intermediate pairwise predictions back as input to refine structure hypotheses, similar to iterative reasoning in LLMs.

claim: Protein folding for monomers is now near-solved (when co-evolution data exists), but multichain assemblies, intrinsically disordered regions, and dynamic state transitions remain open problems requiring new approaches
actionable: Focus structure prediction research on hard generalization cases: proteins without co-evolution history, multi-chain assemblies, flexible loops, and dynamic conformational changes rather than single-chain monomers.

claim: Medicinal chemists require experimental validation (wet-lab hits) to be convinced of ML model outputs; beautiful interfaces and high confidence scores alone do not overcome skepticism
actionable: For ML tools targeting domain experts (medicinal chemists, biologists), invest equally in experimental validation infrastructure and user interfaces; skepticism only breaks with reproducible lab results.

claim: Designing molecules in 'synthesizable space' requires conditioning diffusion models on valid building blocks and synthesis routes; unconditional generation produces chemically infeasible designs
actionable: For molecular design, train generative models on known, synthesizable building blocks and routes; do not use unconditional generation or fine-tune on unrestricted chemical space.
